title: What is JSON
instruction:
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a text format for structured data. Every web API uses it.

```json
{"name": "Alex", "age": 25, "active": true}
```

In Go, `encoding/json` handles everything:
- `json.Marshal(v)` — turns a Go value into JSON bytes
- `json.Unmarshal(data, &v)` — turns JSON bytes into a Go value

Run this. A Go struct becomes a JSON string in three lines.
successMessage: A Go struct became JSON. Notice the keys match the field names exactly — capital letters and all. Next step: control those key names with struct tags.

title: Struct tags — control the JSON keys
instruction:
By default, JSON keys match Go field names exactly. To use lowercase keys (which APIs expect), add struct tags:

```
type User struct {
    Name string `json:"name"`
    Age  int    `json:"age"`
}
```

Add `json:"..."` tags to all three fields of `Product` so the output uses lowercase keys.
hint: Name string `json:"name"`  Price float64 `json:"price"`  InStock bool `json:"in_stock"`

codeChecks.message: Add json:"name" tag to the Name field.
Add json:"price" tag to the Price field.
Add json:"in_stock" tag to the InStock field.
Replace the TODO comments with your struct tags.

successMessage: Lowercase keys, underscore separators — exactly what a REST API expects. Struct tags are how Go bridges the gap between Go naming conventions and JSON naming conventions.

title: omitempty — skip zero values
instruction:
`omitempty` tells the JSON encoder to skip a field if it is its zero value (empty string, 0, false, nil).

```
Age int `json:"age,omitempty"`
```

Useful for optional API fields — send them only when they have a value.

Add `omitempty` to the `Bio` and `Age` tags. Run both users and see which fields appear.

codeChecks.message: Add omitempty to the Age tag: json:"age,omitempty"
Add omitempty to the Bio tag: json:"bio,omitempty"
Replace the TODO comments with the updated tags.

successMessage: Jordan's response has only name — age and bio are zero values so omitempty dropped them. Clean API responses with no unnecessary nulls.

title: Unmarshal — JSON to struct
instruction:
`json.Unmarshal` parses JSON bytes and fills a Go struct.

```
var u User
err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(jsonStr), &u)
```

The `&u` is a pointer — Unmarshal needs to write into your variable.

Unmarshal the second JSON string into a `Product` and print its `Name` and `Price`.
hint: var p Product  err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(jsonStr), &p)  fmt.Println(p.Name, p.Price)

codeChecks.message: Unmarshal jsonStr2 into a Product struct.
Replace the TODO comment with your unmarshal code.

successMessage: JSON string → Go struct. The tags map "name" → Name and "price" → Price automatically. This is how every Go HTTP handler reads request bodies.

title: Unknown fields are ignored
instruction:
When unmarshaling, Go silently ignores JSON keys that do not match any struct field. This is intentional — it lets you parse partial responses without crashing.

Run this. The JSON has four keys but the struct only has two fields. Go fills what it can and ignores the rest.
successMessage: role and created_at were silently ignored. No crash, no error. This is perfect for consuming third-party APIs — you only map the fields you care about.

title: Nested structs in JSON
instruction:
Nested Go structs become nested JSON objects automatically.

Add an `Address` struct with `Street`, `City`, and `Country` fields (all tagged). Nest it inside `User` as `address`. Marshal the user and see the nested JSON.
hint: type Address struct { Street string `json:"street"`; City string `json:"city"`; Country string `json:"country"` }  User has Address Address `json:"address"`

codeChecks.message: Define an Address struct.
Add Address Address `json:"address"` field to User.
Set the Street to "123 Main St".
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Nested struct → nested JSON object. Unmarshal works the same way in reverse — Go fills both levels automatically.

title: Arrays and slices in JSON
instruction:
Go slices become JSON arrays. JSON arrays become Go slices.

`[]string{"Go", "Python"}` → `["Go","Python"]`

Run this. Then add a `Tags []string \`json:"tags"\`` field to `Post`, populate it with two tags, and see them in the output.

codeChecks.message: Add Tags []string `json:"tags"` field to Post.
Include "tutorial" in the Tags slice.
Include "beginner" in the Tags slice.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Slices become JSON arrays effortlessly. The same works for []int, []User, or any slice type — Go handles it all the same way.

title: Maps in JSON
instruction:
`map[string]T` becomes a JSON object with dynamic keys.

Useful when you do not know the field names ahead of time — like user metadata, settings, or arbitrary key-value stores.

Run this. Then add an entry `"theme": "dark"` to the settings map and see it appear in the JSON.

codeChecks.message: Add settings["theme"] = "dark" before marshaling.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Maps become JSON objects with their keys as field names — sorted alphabetically by default. Perfect for dynamic data where you cannot define a struct upfront.

title: MarshalIndent — pretty print
instruction:
`json.MarshalIndent` produces human-readable JSON with line breaks and indentation.

`json.MarshalIndent(v, prefix, indent)`
- prefix — added to the start of each line (usually `""`)
- indent — the indentation string (use `"  "` for two spaces or `"\t"` for tabs)

Switch the marshal call from `json.Marshal` to `json.MarshalIndent` with two-space indentation.

codeChecks.message: Use json.MarshalIndent(u, "", "  ") for pretty printing.
Replace the TODO comment with your MarshalIndent call.

successMessage: Readable, indented JSON. Use this for log files, config files, and debugging. Use json.Marshal for API responses where compact output is preferred.

title: Decoding from a stream
instruction:
`json.NewDecoder` reads JSON from any `io.Reader` — an HTTP response body, a file, a string buffer.

For HTTP APIs this is the right approach: you stream the response directly into your struct without loading everything into memory first.

`decoder.Decode(&v)` reads one JSON value from the stream.

Run this. It decodes three JSON objects from a multi-line string (simulating an HTTP response stream).
successMessage: Three JSON objects, one stream, one decoder. decoder.More() checks if there is more JSON to read. This is how Go HTTP clients decode API responses — efficient, zero extra allocations.

title: Custom marshaling
instruction:
Sometimes the default marshaling is not what you want. Implement `MarshalJSON() ([]byte, error)` on your type to take full control.

Common uses: formatting dates differently, hiding sensitive fields, combining multiple fields.

Run this. `Password` is masked in the JSON output by the custom marshaler. Then change the mask from `"***"` to `"[hidden]"`.

codeChecks.message: Change the password mask to "[hidden]".
Replace the TODO comment with your change.

successMessage: The real password never appears in the JSON. Custom marshalers give you complete control — date formatting, field renaming, computed values, security masking. All without changing the struct.

title: Build an API response builder
hint: func success(data interface{}) string { r := APIResponse{Status: "ok", Data: data}; b, _ := json.MarshalIndent(r, "", "  "); return string(b) }

codeChecks.message: Define an APIResponse struct.
Add json:"status" tag to the Status field.
Use omitempty on Message and/or Data fields.
Define a success() function.
Define a fail() function.
Use json.MarshalIndent for pretty-printed output.
Set status to "ok" in the success function.
Set status to "error" in the fail function.

successMessage: A reusable API response wrapper. Every production Go API uses a pattern like this — consistent shape, clean JSON, omitempty for optional fields. You just built the foundation of a web API.
